5.14: A type: 'So he went down and dipped himself seven times in the Jordan, according to the word of the man of God, and his flesh was restored like the flesh of a little child, and he was clean.' Naaman's healing is one of the OT's most theologically rich type-narratives: (1) the outsider (a Gentile military commander) receives healing denied to insiders (Israelite lepers, Luke 4:27); (2) the healing comes through water — specifically the Jordan, the covenant-crossing water; (3) the means is foolishly simple (wash seven times) — Naaman expects a dramatic prophetic ritual; (4) the result is total cleansing. Jesus's citation of Naaman in Luke 4:27 to explain his own ministry to Nazareth (grace going to unexpected outsiders) and the Baptist's baptism in the Jordan both stand in the Naaman-and-the-Jordan typological stream: the Jordan is the water of covenant-entry and cleansing, fulfilled in baptism into Christ.
5.1: Naaman is introduced with maximum social prestige: commander of the Aramean army, great man (gāḏôl), highly regarded (nᵉśûʾ pānîm — literally “of lifted face” — honored by his master), victorious in battle — YHWH himself is said to have given him the victories. And then: mᵉṣōrāʿ — he was a leper. The structure is the ancient rhetorical form for tragic irony: greatness exhaustively catalogued, then undercut by a single condition that all the greatness cannot address. Wealth, honor, power, military success — and leprosy. Luke 4:27: Jesus cites Naaman specifically as the paradigm case of grace going to unexpected outsiders. The commander of Israel’s enemy army becomes the first recorded Gentile recipient of covenantal healing.
5.2: A little girl from Israel — captured in an Aramean raid, serving Naaman’s wife — carries the word that will heal him. She is unnamed, enslaved, from the enemy people, and a child. The concentric circles of marginalization are total. Yet she is the person through whom the redemptive word travels. 1 Cor 1:27-29: “God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong; God chose what is low and despised in the world.” The word that heals Naaman comes from the lowest person in his household.
5.3: “If only my lord were before the prophet who is in Samaria! He would cure him of his leprosy.” The girl’s testimony is confident and specific: the prophet who is in Samaria can heal. She does not say “I think,” or “perhaps.” Her faith in Elisha’s capacity is unqualified. Acts 8:4: “those who were scattered went about preaching the word” — the captives of the dispersion become the missionaries to the Gentiles. The Samaritan woman at the well (John 4:39: “many Samaritans believed because of the woman’s testimony”) is the NT version of the same pattern: a woman’s witness brings outsiders to the one who heals.
5.4: Naaman reports the girl’s word to the king of Aram — from slave girl to king in one step. The redemptive word travels upward through the social hierarchy by the force of its content, not the status of its carrier. The king of Aram takes the girl’s testimony seriously enough to dispatch a diplomatic mission. Even power listens when desperation is great enough.
5.5: The king sends Naaman with ten talents of silver, six thousand shekels of gold, and ten changes of clothing — enormous wealth intended to purchase the healing. The gift-package attempts to commodify what is freely given. Acts 8:18-19: Simon Magus offers money for the gift of the Spirit. The pattern of those who approach grace as a transaction is consistent: they assume divine favor can be obtained by what is brought rather than by what is received. The wealth Naaman carries will be irrelevant to his healing and a temptation to Gehazi (v20-27).
5.6: The king of Aram writes to the king of Israel: “I have sent my servant Naaman to you, that you may cure him of his leprosy.” The diplomatic channel bypasses the prophet and addresses the king — the institutional approach to grace. The king of Israel is not the healing agent; the prophet is. Approaching God through official channels (political, institutional, hereditary) instead of through the one God has appointed is a recurring human error. John 14:6: “No one comes to the Father except through me.”
5.7: The king of Israel tears his clothes: “Am I God, to give death and life, that this man sends to me to cure a man of his leprosy?” The king’s question is theologically correct — only God can heal leprosy — but practically wrong, because the prophet who speaks for that God is in his kingdom. His tearing of garments misreads the diplomatic letter as a pretext for war; he cannot imagine that YHWH could heal through his prophet. The institutional leader who cannot see the prophetic resource in his own land.
5.8: Elisha hears of the king’s distress and sends word: “Let him come to me, that he may know there is a prophet in Israel.” The purpose of the healing is stated explicitly: wəyēḏaʿ kî yēš nāḇîʾ bəyiśrāʾēl — the knowledge of YHWH’s presence in Israel through his prophet. John 20:31: “these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God.” The miracles are not ends in themselves but revelation — they create knowledge of who YHWH is and where he acts.
5.9: Naaman comes with his horses and chariots — the full military display of a great man — and stands at the door of Elisha’s house. The power and pomp of the Aramean general before the threshold of the prophet’s ordinary house: the great man waiting at the ordinary door is the reversal of expected social power. Jesus: “Behold, I stand at the door and knock” (Rev 3:20) — the divine one waiting at the human door is the ultimate reversal.
5.10: Elisha does not come out but sends a messenger: “Go and wash in the Jordan seven times, and your flesh will be restored, and you shall be clean.” The instruction has three qualities that give offense: (1) Elisha does not come in person; (2) the instruction is insultingly simple; (3) the Jordan — an inferior Israelite river — is specified. The simplicity of the gospel has always been its stumbling block. The means of salvation — faith in the crucified, baptism into his death — appears foolish and inadequate to those who expect something commensurate with their need.
5.11: Naaman is angry: “I expected him to come out to me in person, call on the name of the LORD his God, wave his hand over the place, and cure me of my leprosy.” Naaman has a detailed script for how the healing should work: the prophet appears, invokes YHWH, performs a dramatic gesture. What he expects is a theurgic ritual — the manipulation of divine power by a qualified specialist using proper technique. 1 Cor 1:22-23: “Jews demand signs and Greeks seek wisdom, but we preach Christ crucified.” The cross is foolishness because it refuses the script of power and wisdom; Naaman’s rage at the Jordan instruction is the Gentile form of the same offense.
5.12: “Are not Abana and Pharpar, the rivers of Damascus, better than all the waters of Israel? Could I not wash in them and be clean?” The question is perfectly logical by every natural standard: the Abana and Pharpar (Barada and Awaj rivers) are indeed clear, full rivers; the Jordan is murkier and less impressive. But the healing is not in the water’s quality — it is in the word attached to it. The sacramental principle: the efficacy of baptism is not in the water’s chemistry but in the Word of God that commands and promises. Luther: “it is not the water that does these things, but the Word of God which is in and with the water.”
5.13: His servants appeal: “My father (ʾāḇî), if the prophet had told you to do something great, would you not have done it? How much more, when he says to you, Wash and be clean?” The servants’ logic is the logic of the lesser to the greater. Naaman would have performed any dramatic, costly requirement — why refuse the simple one? The pride that can bear the great sacrifice but cannot bear the humble act is the characteristic human resistance to grace. John 13:8-9: Peter refuses to have his feet washed, then asks for a full bath — the overcorrection of pride brought low.
5.15: “I know that there is no God in all the earth except in Israel.” The Gentile confession after healing: the definitive monotheistic acknowledgment from a pagan military commander who came to buy a healing and instead received a revelation. The structure of Naaman’s conversion — encounter with a captive witness (v3), dramatic encounter with the prophet, healing through simple obedience, personal confession — is the pattern of Gentile conversion in Acts (cf. Acts 10:34-48: Cornelius; Acts 16:27-34: Philippian jailer). Eph 2:12-13: those who were “strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world” are “brought near by the blood of Christ.”
5.16: “As the LORD lives, before whom I stand, I will receive nothing.” Elisha refuses all payment for the healing. The gift of healing that cannot be purchased is the grace that must remain free. Matt 10:8: “You received without paying; give without pay.” The refusal of payment is itself a proclamation: YHWH’s gifts are not for sale. Elisha’s refusal contrasts with every commercial religious system then and now; it anticipates Jesus’s temple cleansing (Matt 21:12-13) — the house of prayer cannot be a house of commerce.
5.17: Naaman requests two mule-loads of Israelite earth to build an altar for YHWH at Damascus — he will worship YHWH only on Israelite soil. The earth-theology is transitional: in the ANE, gods were considered to have territorial jurisdiction; Naaman imports the territory of YHWH’s land. The request is simultaneously theologically limited (YHWH still geographically bound in Naaman’s mind) and theologically revolutionary (a Gentile worshiping YHWH in his own country). John 4:21-24: Jesus dismantles the territorial theology entirely — “the hour is coming when you will worship the Father neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem... true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and truth.” Naaman’s two mule-loads of earth point toward the worship that no longer needs any particular soil.
5.18: “When my master goes into the house of Rimmon to worship there, leaning on my arm, and I bow myself in the house of Rimmon — when I bow myself in the house of Rimmon, may YHWH pardon your servant in this one matter.” The Rimmon problem is the first missionary pastoral dilemma: the Gentile convert who must function in a context of unavoidable idolatry. Naaman is not asking permission to worship Rimmon; he is asking pardon for an unavoidable bodily participation in a ceremony he will not internally affirm. Elisha’s “go in peace” without qualification is remarkable pastoral accommodation. The Apostolic Council (Acts 15) wrestles with the same question: what does the Gentile convert owe to the full Torah? The answer in both cases: not the full burden, but the core of covenantal faith.
5.19: “Go in peace.” — lēk lᵉšālôm. The word of release given to the healed Gentile. The same word that ends Elijah’s encounter with the widow (1 Kgs 17:23: “See, your son lives”) and will be spoken by Jesus at departures of healing (“Your faith has saved you; go in peace,” Luke 7:50; 8:48). The šālôm word is the blessing that accompanies those whom YHWH has restored and released.
5.20: Gehazi’s interior monologue: “My master has let this Aramean off too lightly by not accepting from him what he brought.” The resentment of grace extended to the outsider — the elder son’s reaction when the father throws a feast for the prodigal (Luke 15:28-30: “I have served you these many years, and you never gave me a young goat... but when this son of yours came, who has devoured your property with prostitutes, you killed the fattened calf for him”). Gehazi’s “too lightly” is the disciple’s refusal of the logic of grace: that the gift should be given freely, without extraction.
5.21: Gehazi runs after Naaman. When Naaman sees him coming, he steps down from his chariot to meet him — the just-healed Gentile showing honor to the prophet’s servant. The newly converted Naaman is more gracious in his encounter with the greedy servant than the servant is in his exploitation of the convert. The convert who was the recipient of grace acts with the generosity the disciple lacks.
5.22: Gehazi lies to Naaman: “My master has sent me” — invoking Elisha’s authority he does not have, for a need he has invented. The lie compounds the greed with false representation of the prophet. Judas: “What will you give me if I deliver him to you?” (Matt 26:15) — the disciple who monetizes his access to the master. The pattern of the inner-circle disciple whose proximity to the grace-giver becomes an occasion for exploitation.
5.23: Naaman gives generously — two talents of silver and two changes of clothing, more than Gehazi asked. The healed man’s generosity contrasts with the servant’s scheme. Naaman, the recent convert, gives from gratitude; Gehazi, the long-serving insider, takes from greed. The inversion of the expected generosity-calculus.
5.24: Gehazi takes the gifts to the hill, stores them in the house, and dismisses the men who carried them. The concealment — putting the goods away before returning to Elisha — is the action of one who knows the act will not bear scrutiny. Achan after the Jericho ban (Josh 7:21: “I saw among the spoil... I coveted them and took them... they are hidden in the earth inside my tent”) — the pattern of the covetous theft hidden from the community.
5.25: Gehazi returns and stands before Elisha as if nothing has happened. “Your servant has not gone anywhere.” The lie direct — the denial of a specific action to the one who already knows. Peter before the servant girl (Mark 14:68: “I neither know nor understand what you mean”); Ananias and Sapphira before Peter (Acts 5:3-4: “you have not lied to men but to God”). The lie told to the prophet is the lie told to YHWH himself.
5.26: “Did not my heart go with you when the man stepped down from his chariot to meet you?” — Elisha’s prophetic knowledge encompasses what Gehazi thought was private. Then the rebuke: “Is this a time to receive money and garments, olive orchards and vineyards, sheep and oxen, male servants and female servants?” The expanding list of things that silver can buy — the catalogue of covetousness — shows how the first acquisition opens the imagination to all that follows. 1 Tim 6:10: “the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil.” Gehazi’s single act of greed opens the door to a life organized around acquisition.
5.27: “Therefore the leprosy of Naaman shall cling to you and to your descendants forever.” Gehazi goes out white as snow with leprosy — the disease that left Naaman transfers to the servant who profited from his healing. The judgment is exact: Gehazi exploited the grace given to a leper; now he receives the condition from which that grace had freed the Gentile. The leprosy “forever” (lᵉʿôlām) is the permanent consequence of the permanent covenant violation. The Judas parallel: “It would have been better for that man if he had not been born” (Matt 26:24) — the insider who exploits the master’s gift inherits a judgment proportional to the privilege he betrayed.

6.17: Elisha’s prayer — YHWH pᵉqaḥ nāʾ ʾet ʿênāyw wᵉyirʾeh, ‘YHWH, please open his eyes that he may see’ — and the servant’s vision of the mountain filled with horses and chariots of fire is the OT’s clearest narrative demonstration of the reality of the invisible. The servant’s natural eyes see the Aramean army; his opened eyes see YHWH’s army. The greater reality is invisible; what appears threatening is actually surrounded by what cannot be seen. 2 Cor 4:18 names the NT principle: mē skopountōn hēmōn ta blepomena alla ta mē blepomena; ta gar blepomena proskaira, ta de mē blepomena aiōnia — ‘we look not to the things that are seen but to the things that are unseen. For the things that are seen are transient, but the things that are unseen are eternal.’ Heb 11:1 defines faith precisely in terms of this structural dynamic: estin de pistis elpizomenōn hypostasis, pragmatōn elegchos ou blepomenōn — ‘faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.’ The servant’s opened eyes are the narrative form of what faith operates in: the greater reality that surrounds the threatening visible, and that prayer accesses. The apostolic prayer-pattern of Eph 1:18 (‘having the eyes of your hearts enlightened’) is the NT form of the same petition: open his eyes that he may see.
6.22: Elisha’s instruction to feed the captive Aramean army rather than kill them — śim lāhem leḥem ûmayim, ‘set bread and water before them’ — is one of the OT’s most striking enactments of the enemy-love principle. The king of Israel asks whether to kill; Elisha says: feed. The result (v23): ‘the Aramean raiders no more came into the land of Israel.’ The enemy fed becomes the peaceful neighbor. Paul in Rom 12:20 cites Prov 25:21-22: ean peinaō ho echthros sou, psōmize auton; ean dipsa, potizon auton — ‘if your enemy is hungry, feed him; if he is thirsty, give him something to drink; for by so doing you will heap burning coals on his head.’ The Elisha-feeding narrative is the OT’s enacted form of this principle: the enemy army given bread and water departs in peace. Jesus in Matt 5:44 (‘love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you’) names the disposition; Elisha at Dothan provides the OT narrative illustration. The feeding of enemies at the prophet’s table also anticipates the kingdom-table of Luke 14:12-14, where Jesus instructs precisely not to invite friends but to invite those who cannot repay — the Elisha-table becomes a type of the eschatological banquet that reverses all expected social hierarchies.
6.1: The prophets' community asks Elisha for space to build by the Jordan: hannāqôm ʾăšer ʾănaḥnû yōšᵉḇîm šām lᵉpānêḵā ṣar mimmennu — “the place where we dwell before you is too small for us.” The expansion of the prophetic community signals blessing and growth under Elisha's ministry. The detail that the students build with their own hands points to a community of voluntary discipleship organized around the prophet — anticipating the pattern of itinerant teachers gathering disciples (cf. Jesus's disciples, Matt 4:18-22).
6.2: nēlᵉḵāh nāʾ ʾel hayYardēn wᵉniqqᵉḥāh miššām ʾîš qôrāh ʾeḥāt wᵉnaʿăśeh šām māqôm lāšeḇet šām — let us go to the Jordan and each take a log and build a place to dwell. Elisha's agreement to accompany them (ʾĕlēḵ) models the leader-accompanies-disciples pattern. His presence transforms what would be ordinary labor into a scene of miraculous provision.
6.3: One of the prophets urges Elisha to come with them: hôʾel nāʾ ûlēḵ ʾet-ʿăḇādêḵā — please go with your servants. The community functions as disciples of a master (ʾăḇādîm, servants), not merely colleagues. Elisha consents: ʾēlēḵ — he goes. The master's willingness to be present at the disciples' ordinary work is characteristic of the servant-prophet pattern.
6.4: Elisha goes with them to the Jordan. As they are cutting wood, one of them loses the iron axe-head: wayyippol habbarzel ʾel-hammāyim — the iron fell into the water. The problem is compounded: wᵉhûʾ šāʾûl — and it was borrowed. The borrowed iron creates both a practical and ethical problem: the man cannot replace what is not his. The narrative's interest in the economic predicament of borrowed equipment grounds the miracle in everyday consequence.
6.5: The man cries out to Elisha: ʾăhāh ʾădōnî wᵉhûʾ šāʾûl — “Alas, my lord, and it was borrowed!” The double waw of distress in ʾăhāh (ah/alas) is the cry of someone caught between loss and obligation. The problem — a heavy iron tool sunk in the Jordan — is precisely the kind of irreversible situation (iron sinks, water flows) where only a miracle can restore what was lost. Elisha asks simply: ʾannāh nāpal — where did it fall?
6.6: Elisha cuts a stick (wayyiqaṣ ʿēṣ) and throws it at the spot where the iron fell — wayyišlaḵ šāmmāh wayyāṣep habbārzel — and the iron floated up (ṣûp, to swim/float). The miracle of making iron float inverts natural law: iron is among the heaviest common metals. Throwing wood (which floats) to cause iron to float creates a typological picture — some interpreters read the wood-thrown-into-water causing life from death as a type of the Cross (wood-in-water causing restoration). Elisha says: qah-lāḵ ʾōtô — take it up for yourself.
6.7: wayyišlaḥ yādô wayyiqqāḥēhû — the man stretched out his hand and took it. The miracle of the floating axe completes the Elisha miracle cycle (cf. 2 Kgs 2-5) showing the prophet's mastery over nature. The restoration of what was lost (the borrowed iron, returned to its owner) anticipates the broader redemptive theme of the Elisha narratives: the prophet restores what was taken — life, oil, food, and now a tool. Jesus as the greater prophet who restores what was lost (zētein kai sōzein to apolōlos, “to seek and save the lost,” Luke 19:10) stands in this lineage.
6.8: The narrative shifts to the Syro-Aramean military campaigns. The king of Aram instructs his commanders: bimqôm pelōnî ʾalmōnî taḥanōtî — “at such-and-such a place, encamp.” This is a placeholder formula (literally “so-and-so, thus-and-so”) for undisclosed locations. Elisha's foreknowledge of these secret positions is the theological pivot of the passage: the prophet has access to knowledge that belongs to the divine council.
6.9: Elisha sends word to the Israelite king: hišāmēr mēʿăḇōr hamāqôm hazzeh kî-šām ʾărām nōḥătîm — guard yourself from passing through this place, for Aram is going down there. The prophet functions as Israel's intelligence network — not through human espionage but through prophetic revelation. The pattern of God revealing hidden plans to his prophets (Amos 3:7: lōʾ yaʿăśeh ʾădōnāy YHWH dāḇār kî ʾim gālāh sôdô ʾel ʿăḇādāyw hannᵉḇiʾîm) is demonstrated practically here.
6.10: The king of Israel sends to the locations Elisha identifies — more than once or twice (lōʾ ʾaḥat wᵉlōʾ šetāyim) — and each time escapes. The repeated pattern of prophetic forewarning versus Aramean ambush suggests an ongoing surveillance-countermeasure dynamic. From the Aramean perspective, there is a leak in their security. The king of Aram suspects a traitor: mî mišellānû ʾel melek yiśrāʾēl — who of us is betraying us to the king of Israel?
6.11: The Aramean king's heart (lēb melek ʾărām) is troubled (wayyissāʿēr — stirred up, disturbed) over the intelligence failures. He suspects his own commanders. One of his servants answers: lōʾ ʾădōnî hammélek kî ʾĕlîšāʿ hannāḇîʾ ʾăšer bᵉyiśrāʾēl yaggîd lᵉmelek yiśrāʾēl ʾet hadᵉḇārîm ʾăšer tᵉdabbēr bᵉḥădar miškāḇêḵā — “None, my lord the king; but Elisha the prophet in Israel tells the king of Israel the words you speak in your bedroom.” The prophet hears what is spoken in secret chambers — a capacity attributed in the NT to Jesus: “Nothing is covered up that will not be revealed, or hidden that will not be known” (Luke 12:2).
6.12: The servant's report: Elisha the prophet tells Israel's king ʾet hadᵉḇārîm ʾăšer tᵉdabbēr bᵉḥădar miškāḇêḵā — the very words spoken in your bedroom. This claim of prophetic omniscience is remarkable in its scope — not just battle plans but private speech. The NT applies this same motif to Jesus, who knows what people think (John 2:25; Mark 2:8) and hears what is said in private. The Elisha narrative prepares an interpretive framework for this kind of prophetic/divine knowledge.
6.13: The king of Aram commands: lᵉḵû ûrᵉʾû ʾêkōh hûʾ wᵉʾešlaḥ wᵉʾeqqāḥēhû — go and see where he is, and I will send and seize him. The move from intelligence-sharing to active capture marks an escalation: the prophet is now a military target. Elisha is located at Dothan. The arrest-of-the-prophet pattern — authorities threatened by prophetic truth seeking to silence it — recurs throughout the OT (Jer 38:4-13) and reaches its climax in the arrest of Jesus (Matt 26:47-56).
6.14: The Aramean king sends horses, chariots, and a great army (sûsîm wᵉreḵeḇ wᵉḥayil kāḇēd) to surround Dothan by night. The military force deployed against a single prophet is a measure of how threatening prophetic truth-telling was to Aramean military power. The night siege creates the dramatic context for the servant's morning terror and the heavenly vision of v17 (present).
6.15: The servant rises early and sees the surrounding army: ḥayil sāḇîḇ hāʿîr wᵉsûs wāreḵeḇ — a great army surrounding the city, horses and chariots. wayyōʾmer ʾelāyw naʿar ʾăhāh ʾădōnî mah naʿăśeh — the servant cries, “Alas, my lord, what shall we do?” The servant's fear is entirely reasonable from the natural perspective. His inability to see the heavenly army represents the normal human condition: we perceive the physical threat but not the divine protection. Elisha's response establishes the fundamental theological principle of the scene.
6.16: wayyōʾmer ʾal-tîrāʾ kî rabîm ʾăšer ʾittānû mēʾăšer ʾittām — “Do not fear, for those who are with us are more than those who are with them.” This verse is among the most quoted in Christian theology for the concept of spiritual protection and divine accompaniment. The arithmetic is invisible to the servant: the more (rabîm) on YHWH's side exceeds the visible enemy. Paul's “if God is for us, who can be against us?” (Rom 8:31) is the NT equivalent. Jesus's promise of his presence with the disciples (Matt 28:20) grounds the same reality in a permanent rather than episodic guarantee.
6.18: As the Arameans come down, Elisha prays: hak-nāʾ ʾet-haggôy hazzeh bassanwērîm — “Strike this nation with blindness.” The Hebrew sanwērîm (blinding, related to Gen 19:11 at Sodom) denotes a mental-physical disorientation — struck unable to see clearly, confused. The prayer is answered immediately. This prophetic bestowal of blindness and sight parallels Elisha's earlier raising of the dead child (ch4): the prophet exercises authority over perception/death as divine instruments. The NT parallel is Paul's blinding on the Damascus road (Acts 9:3-9) — an encounter that simultaneously blinds physically and opens spiritual sight.
6.19: Elisha tells the blinded Arameans: lōʾ-zeh hadereḵ wᵉlōʾ-zōʾt hāʿîr lᵉḵû ʾaḥărāy — “This is not the way, and this is not the city; follow me, and I will lead you to the man you are seeking.” Elisha leads the men who came to capture him straight into the hands of the Israelite king — a reversal of captivity where the hunter becomes the captive without knowing it. The irony is profound: the prophet who was sought becomes the guide. Jesus, when soldiers come to arrest him in Gethsemane, also takes control of the encounter — “I am he” (John 18:5-6) causing the soldiers to fall back.
6.20: When they reach Samaria, Elisha prays again: YHWH pᵉqaḥ ʾet-ʿênê ʾēlleh wᵉyirʾû — “YHWH, open the eyes of these men, that they may see.” The same opening-of-eyes prayer that was prayed for the servant in v17 (that he might see the heavenly army) is now prayed for the enemy soldiers (that they might see where they are). Both blindness and sight are in the prophet's gift, through prayer. The enemies see themselves surrounded — in Samaria, Israel's capital. The contrast with the original plan (capture Elisha) and the outcome (the capturers are captured) illustrates the divine reversal theme central to Elisha's ministry.
6.21: The king of Israel asks Elisha: hāʾakkeh ʾakkeh ʾăḇî — “Shall I strike them down, my father?” The king's address of Elisha as ʾāḇî (my father) is the same address used by Elisha himself for Elijah at his translation (2 Kgs 2:12). The prophet is the nation's spiritual father — the one whose guidance determines the nation's choices. The king is ready to execute the captured enemy soldiers; Elisha's answer will counter this impulse with a radical form of mercy.
6.23: Elisha instructs: śim leḥem wāmayim wᵉyōḵlû wᵉyišᵉtû wᵉyēlᵉḵû ʾel ʾădōnîhem — set bread and water before them that they may eat and drink, and go to their master. The king provides a great feast (kᵉrāh gᵉḏôlāh) and then releases them. wᵉlōʾ yāsᵉpû ʿôd gᵉdûdê ʾărām lāḇôʾ bᵉʾereṣ yiśrāʾēl — and the raiding parties of Aram no longer came into the land of Israel. The feeding and release of enemies produces peace. This is Prov 25:21-22 (“if your enemy is hungry, give him bread”) enacted in narrative — Paul quotes it in Rom 12:20 as the principle of overcoming evil with good. The morality of the episode prefigures the Sermon on the Mount's enemy-love ethic (Matt 5:43-48).
6.24: Despite the peace won through mercy (v23), Ben-hadad of Aram later besieges Samaria — a new campaign suggesting either a different Ben-hadad or a later escalation. wayyiṣōr ʿal-Šomrôn — and besieged Samaria. The siege creates famine so severe that a donkey's head sold for eighty pieces of silver (šᵉmōnîm kesef) and dove's dung for five. The inverted economy of siege famine — worthless items (donkey's head) priced as luxuries — is a covenant-curse reversal: Deut 28:53-57 specifically threatens siege-induced cannibalism as the consequence of covenant violation.
6.25: The extreme famine prices: a donkey's head for eighty shekels, a qab (small measure) of dove's dung for five shekels. Donkey's head was non-kosher (Lev 11:4) — its consumption indicates the complete breakdown of dietary law observance under extreme hunger. Dove's dung is either literal (used as fuel or food) or a derogatory name for a cheap vegetable. The siege-famine sequence will serve as the backdrop for Elisha's impossible prophecy in ch7 and its miraculous fulfillment.
6.26: A woman cries to the king for justice as he passes on the wall: hôšîʿāh ʾădōnî hammélek — “Save me, my lord the king!” The cry for royal salvation (yāšaʿ) during siege-famine creates the context for the cannibalism narrative. The king's helpless response — lōʾ yôšîʿēḵ YHWH mēʾayin ʾôšîʿēḵ — “YHWH has not saved you, from where shall I save you?” — acknowledges the theological dimension of the siege: it is YHWH's judgment, not merely Aramean military power.
6.27: The king asks: mah-lāḵ — what is your trouble? The formal inquiry before judgment follows legal procedure. The woman's complaint reveals the horror of siege-famine: an agreement to boil and eat each other's children in sequence. The cannibalism of children — specifically a mother-and-neighbor agreement to consume their own sons — is the exact fulfillment of the siege curse in Deut 28:53-57, where YHWH threatens that the most tender man and the most gentle woman will eat their own children in the distress of siege.
6.28: The woman's report: ʾimrāh lî ʾēt bᵉnēḵ wᵉnōḵlennû hayyôm ûʾet bᵉnî nōḵal māḥār — she said to me, give your son that we may eat him today, and tomorrow we will eat my son. The agreement represents the utter dissolution of the most fundamental human bond — maternal protection of children. The Deuteronomic curse is fulfilled literally. The narrative is placed precisely here — after the peace achieved through Elisha's mercy-feeding of enemies — to demonstrate that Israel's violence is directed inward when it should be outward in enemy-love.
6.29: wanᵉḇaššel ʾet bᵉnî wanōḵlēhû — so we boiled my son and ate him. The neighbor then hid her son (wayyaṣpᵉnēhû). The horror of the narrative is presented without editorial comment — the narrator records the worst outcome of siege-famine without softening. The king hears this and tears his garments in mourning (wayyiqraʿ ʾet-bᵉgādāyw). The rending of garments by the king connects this to prophetic mourning gestures throughout Kings and to the NT tearing of the temple veil (Matt 27:51) — the garment of the old order torn in judgment.
6.30: As the king walks on the wall, the people see that sackcloth (śaq) is on his body beneath his garments — he has been in private mourning. The sight of the king in mourning garments publicly reveals the full depth of the national crisis. Kings publicly mourning signal the collapse of the protective order — the king cannot protect, cannot provide, cannot save. The inadequacy of human kingship in the face of covenantal judgment is the DH's running critique, answered only by the messianic king who actually saves.
6.31: The king vows: kōh-yaʿăśeh lî ʾĕlōhîm wᵉḵōh yôsîp ʾim yaʿĂmod rōʾš ʾĔlîšāʿ ben-Šāpāṭ ʿālāyw hayyôm — “may God do so to me and more also if the head of Elisha son of Shaphat stands on him today.” The king turns his grief into rage against the prophet — blaming Elisha for the siege. This is the classic pattern of shooting the messenger: the prophet who announces judgment is blamed for the judgment. Ahab tried the same with Elijah (1 Kgs 18:17; 21:20). Jesus is similarly blamed for the consequences of his message.
6.32: Elisha is sitting in his house with elders when the king's messenger comes. He perceives the messenger coming before he arrives: hinnēh ʾăšer šālaḥ hammélek lahāsîr ʾet-rōʾšî — the king has sent to take his head. Elisha warns the elders: hold the door against the messenger, then press his foot behind the messenger. Elisha's foreknowledge of the assassination attempt (knowing the king's intent before the messenger arrives) parallels Jesus's foreknowledge of his own betrayal (John 13:21-27).
6.33: The king himself arrives after his messenger — wayyōʾmer mah-ʾîntîn laYHWH ʿôd — “This trouble is from YHWH; why should I wait for YHWH any longer?” The king's theological despair is the exact inverse of faith: he correctly identifies the source of the trouble (YHWH's judgment) but draws the wrong conclusion (abandonment of hope). Elisha's response in ch7:1 will counter this despair with a specific, timed promise of abundance. The movement from “why wait for YHWH?” to miraculous provision within 24 hours is the narrative's theological point: the darkest moment precedes the dawn of salvation.

7.9: The four lepers’ recognition of their obligation — yôm bᵉśôrāh hûʾ, ‘this day is a day of good news’ — is the OT’s clearest narrative application of the gospel-obligation. The lepers have news that will save the starving city; withholding it out of self-interest would be a punishable offense. The word bᵉśôrāh (good news, gospel) is the Hebrew root behind the LXX’s euangelion. Isa 52:7 (‘how beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news [mᵉḇaśśēr], who publishes peace, who brings good news of happiness’) applies this vocabulary to the herald of eschatological restoration; Paul in Rom 10:15 cites Isa 52:7 directly as the warrant for apostolic proclamation: pōs de kēryx sōsin ean mē apostalosōsin; kathōs gegraptai, hōs hōraioi hoi podes tōn euangelizomenōn agatha. The four lepers are the OT’s unexpected heralds — socially excluded, marginally positioned, but carriers of the word that saves. The pattern recurs in the resurrection: the first heralds of the empty tomb are women (Luke 24:9-10), socially undervalued as witnesses; the apostolic proclamation begins with the socially unlikely, as did the lepers’ announcement at the city gate. The lepers’ ‘this is a day of good news and we are keeping silent’ is the OT’s starkest statement of the obligation that attaches to received grace.
7.1: Elisha's word of impossible abundance: šimʿû dᵉḇar YHWH — hear the word of YHWH. kōh ʾāmar YHWH kāʿēt māḥār seʾāh sōlet bᵉšeqel ûsātayim śᵉʿōrîm bᵉšeqel bᵉšaʿar Šomrôn — tomorrow about this time a seah of fine flour for a shekel, and two seahs of barley for a shekel, at the gate of Samaria. Cheap food at the city gate within 24 hours — from the depths of siege-cannibalism famine (ch6:28-29) to market abundance. The specificity of the promise (next day, exact price, specific location) makes it prophetically falsifiable. Jesus's feeding of the five thousand (John 6) operates on similar prophetic-miraculous logic: impossible abundance given by the word of God's representative.
7.2: The king's officer (šālîš) on whose arm the king leaned responds with scorn: hinnēh YHWH ʿōśeh ʾărubbôt baššāmayim hăyihyeh haddāḇār hazzeh — “even if YHWH were to make windows in the heavens, could this thing happen?” The hypothetical of ʾărubbôt baššāmayim (floodgates/windows in the sky) echoes Gen 7:11 (the opened windows of heaven at the Flood) as an impossibility benchmark. Elisha's response is judicial: hinnᵉḵā rōʾeh bᵉʿênêḵā wᵉmiššam lōʾ tōḵal — you will see it with your eyes but shall not eat of it. Unbelief is not disproven by seeing; it forecloses participation in the blessing. Thomas's unbelief (John 20:25) and the crowd's unbelief at the feeding (John 6:30) follow the same dynamic: they see but do not receive.
7.3: Four lepers sit at the entrance of the city gate: mᵉṣōrāʿîm hāyû pétaḥ-haššāʿar — they were excluded from the city by their leprosy (Lev 13:46). Their deliberation — lāmmāh ʾănaḥnû yōšᵉḇîm pōh ʿad mĕtnû — “Why are we sitting here until we die?” — is the pragmatic reasoning of the marginalized. The lepers are the last people anyone would expect to bring good news. They have no stake in either the city or the Aramean camp.
7.4: The lepers reason: if we go into the city, we die of famine; if we sit here, we die. wᵉʿattāh lᵉḵû wᵉnipᵉlāh ʾel maḥănēh ʾărām — so let us go over to the Aramean camp. If they let us live, we live; if they kill us, we die. The logic is pure desperation: death is certain either way, so try the option with the slim chance of survival. This pragmatic surrender to the enemy paradoxically becomes the mechanism of national deliverance. The lepers' willingness to go where no one else would go mirrors the pattern of unexpected instruments of salvation throughout Scripture.
7.5: The four lepers go to the edge of the Aramean camp at twilight (binesep hāʿareḇ): wayyāqûmû binesep hāʿareḇ lāḇôʾ ʾel maḥănēh ʾărām wayyāḇōʾû ʿad qᵉṣēh maḥănēh ʾărām wᵉhinnēh ʾên šām ʾîš — they came to the edge of the camp and behold, there was no one there. The discovery of the abandoned camp is a moment of pure wonder — the expected army is absent.
7.6: The theological explanation: wᵉʾădōnāy hišmîaʿ ʾet maḥănēh ʾărām qôl reḵeḵeḇ qôl sûs qôl ḥayil gādôl — the Lord had made the Aramean army hear a sound of chariots, horses, and a great army. They concluded (wayyōʾmᵉrû): Israel's king has hired the Hittites and Egyptians against us. The auditory miracle — YHWH creating the sound of a massive approaching army — panicked the Arameans into flight. The divine weapon is sound, not sword: YHWH fights the battle through confusion rather than force (cf. Judg 4:15; 7:22). The New Covenant equivalent is the sound of a mighty rushing wind at Pentecost (Acts 2:2) — YHWH's presence announced acoustically.
7.7: wayyāqûmû wayyānûsû bannesep wayyaʿazbû ʾet ʾōhᵉlêhem wᵉʾet sûsêhem wᵉʾet ḥămōrêhem hammahăneh kaʾăšer hîʾ wayyānūsû ʾel napšām — they rose and fled at twilight and abandoned their tents, horses, donkeys, and the camp as it was, and fled for their lives. The abandonment of everything — tents, horses, donkeys — in haste to flee recalls the Exodus plunder (Exod 12:36): Israel went out with Egyptian wealth that the Egyptians left behind in their urgency. The Samaritan lepers now find what Israel found at the Exodus — abundance left by the departing enemy.
7.8: The lepers enter the camp: wayyāḇōʾû hamᵉṣōrāʿîm hāʾēlleh ʾad qᵉṣēh hammahăneh wayyāḇōʾû ʾel-ʾōhel ʾeḥāt wayyōḵlû wayyišᵉtû wayyiśʾû miššam kesef wᵉzāhāḇ ûḇᵉgādîm wayyēlᵉḵû wayyaṭmᵉnû — they eat, drink, carry off silver, gold, and garments, and hide them. The lepers' first response to found treasure is personal consumption and hoarding. This is the natural human response to unexpected abundance. But their conscience brings them back to the need to share the news.
7.10: The lepers go to the city gatekeepers and report: bāʾnû ʾel maḥănēh ʾărām wᵉhinnēh ʾên šām ʾîš wᵉqôl ʾādām kî ʾim sûs ʾāsûr wāḥămôr ʾāsûr wᵉʾōhālîm kᵉʾăšer hēm — we came to the Aramean camp and there was no one there, no human voice — only horses and donkeys tied, and tents as they were. The announcement from the city gate echoes Isa 52:7: mah-nāwû ʿal hehārîm raglê mᵉḇaśśēr — “how beautiful on the mountains are the feet of the one who brings good news.” The lepers become evangelists — their feet carry the good news first to the city gate, then through the gatekeepers to the king.
7.11: The gatekeepers call out (wayyiqrᵉʾû haššōʿᵉrîm wayyaggîdû bêt hammélek pᵉnîmāh) and report to the royal palace. The chain of communication — lepers to gatekeepers to king — mirrors the pattern of gospel spread: the message originates with unlikely witnesses and propagates through institutional channels. The Samaritan woman (John 4) follows the same pattern: she who was least expected brings the news of the Messiah to her whole city.
7.12: The king rises at night and suspects a trap: ʾaggîdāh nāʾ lāḵem ʾēt ʾăšer ʿāśû lānû ʾărām — “I will tell you what the Arameans have done to us.” He knows we are hungry; they have abandoned the camp to draw us out; when we go, they will ambush us. The king's paranoia — interpreting miraculous provision as a military ruse — is theologically significant: he cannot receive grace without suspecting manipulation. The instinct to interpret divine gift as trap is a form of theological paralysis.
7.13: A servant suggests sending five horses to check: even if they die, it is no worse than the people dying in the city. The pragmatic argument — the risk of verification is no worse than the cost of inaction — persuades the king. Two chariot teams are sent (wayyiqḥû šᵉnê reḵeḇ sûsîm). The willingness to risk a small amount to verify the promised salvation parallels the demand for evidence before belief that recurs in the NT (John 20:25; Matt 27:40-42).
7.14: The two chariot teams go to the Jordan following the trail of discarded garments and equipment (bᵉgādîm ûḵēlîm ʾăšer hišlîḵû ʾărām bᵉḥipzām) left by the fleeing Arameans. The trail of abandoned equipment confirms the lepers' report. The physical evidence — clothes and gear strewn on the road — makes the miraculous flight visible and traceable. The abandoned equipment is the material residue of divine intervention.
7.15: The messengers return and report to the king. The people go out and plunder the Aramean camp: wayyiśʾû hāʿām wayyābōzzû ʾet maḥănēh ʾărām — and the people went out and plundered the Aramean camp. wayhî seʾāh sōlet bᵉšeqel ûsātayim śᵉʿōrîm bᵉšeqel kidbar YHWH — a seah of fine flour for a shekel and two seahs of barley for a shekel, according to the word of YHWH. The formula of prophetic fulfillment (kidbar YHWH) is the narrator's emphatic claim: the specific price prophecy of v1 has been fulfilled exactly. No word of YHWH fails.
7.16: The people go out and plunder: wayyābōzzû ʾet maḥănēh ʾărām — they plundered the Aramean camp. The goods of the defeated enemy flow to the besieged city — a complete reversal of the siege economy. Famine to abundance within one day fulfills Elisha's prophecy (v1). The reversal is so rapid and so complete that it can only be attributed to YHWH's direct action. The same sudden reversal of fortunes — from desperate hunger to miraculous provision — characterizes the feeding of the five thousand (John 6:11) and the eschatological banquet (Isa 25:6-8).
7.17: The king appoints the unbelieving officer (from v2) to manage the city gate — and the people trampled him in the gate and he died (wayyirmeśû-ʾōtô hāʿām baššaʿar wayyāmōt). This fulfills Elisha's prophetic word in v2: hinnᵉḵā rōʾeh bᵉʿênêḵā wᵉmiššam lōʾ tōḵal — you will see it with your eyes but not eat of it. The officer sees the abundance but does not participate in it — trampled at the very gate where good news arrives. Unbelief in the face of prophetic promise does not prevent the fulfillment; it only excludes the unbeliever from participating in the blessing.
7.18: The narrator restates Elisha's prophecy from v1 verbatim for the fulfillment-confirmation formula: wayhî kidbar ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm ʾăšer dibber ʾel hammélek — it came to pass according to the word of the man of God that he spoke to the king. The double narration (prophecy + fulfillment notation) is the DH's standard technique for prophetic fulfillment certification. Every major prophecy in Kings receives this confirmation stamp.
7.19: The officer's doubting speech is restated: wᵉhinnēh YHWH ʿōśeh ʾărubbôt baššāmayim hăyihyeh kaddāḇār hazzeh — even if YHWH made windows in the heaven, could this be? This repetition creates the fulfillment-confirmation structure: the exact words of unbelief are quoted again to show they have been answered. The narrative technique highlights that prophetic words — and doubting responses — are remembered and evaluated against what actually happened.
7.20: wayyehî-lô ḵēn wayyirmeśû-ʾōtô hāʿām baššaʿar wayyāmōt — and so it happened to him; the people trampled him in the gate and he died. The double fulfillment — abundance arrived (positive), unbeliever trampled (negative) — forms a bipartite prophetic fulfillment: the word of YHWH produces both salvation and judgment simultaneously. The same structure operates at Jesus's first coming: his words produce both resurrection and condemnation (John 5:24-29), both the opened eyes of the blind and the confirmed blindness of those who claim to see (John 9:39).

8.11: Elisha’s weeping as he gazes at Hazael — seeing what Hazael will do to Israel — combines prophetic knowledge of coming judgment with the prophet’s grief over it. The man of God who announces judgment is not unmoved by it; the commission to speak the word of YHWH does not insulate the prophet from the weight of what that word contains. Luke 19:41-44 narrates Jesus weeping over Jerusalem: kai hōs ēggisen, idōn tēn polin eklausen ep’ autēn, ‘and when he drew near and saw the city, he wept over it, saying, “Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace!”’ — and proceeds to announce the city’s destruction. The weeping prophet who announces judgment is the OT type that Jesus inhabits and fulfills in its deepest form: the one who is the judgment of God (John 5:22: ‘the Father judges no one but has given all judgment to the Son’) is also the one who weeps over the judgment falling on those who reject it. Elisha weeps because he foresees what Hazael will do; Jesus weeps because he foresees what Rome will do — and in both cases the weeping is the prophet’s authentic grief, not performance. Jer 8:21-9:1 (‘for the wound of the daughter of my people is my heart wounded; is there no balm in Gilead?’) is the same prophetic structure, connecting Elisha, Jeremiah, and Jesus in a single pattern of judgment-announcing grief.
8.1: Elisha warns the Shunammite woman (cf. 2 Kgs 4:8-37) that YHWH has called for a seven-year famine (ûgūr baʾăšer tāgûrî kî qārāʾ YHWH lārāʿāḇ wᵉgam bāʾ ʾel hāʾāreṣ šeḇaʿ šānîm) — leave and sojourn wherever you can. The Elisha-Shunammite narrative from ch4 (resurrection of her son) is resumed here with the prophet's care for her material welfare. The seven-year famine matches the pattern of Gen 41 (Joseph's seven-year famine) — Elisha's prophetic forewarning enables the Shunammite to avoid the curse before it arrives, just as Joseph's interpretation enabled Egypt to prepare.
8.2: The woman acts on Elisha's word: wattāqom hāʾiššāh wattaʿaś kidbar ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — she arose and did according to the man of God's word. She leaves for Philistia with her household and sojourns there seven years. Obedience to prophetic word preserves her through the famine. The contrast with unbelieving characters throughout the Elisha cycle is pointed: those who act on prophetic word receive blessing; those who doubt receive nothing or worse.
8.3: At the end of seven years she returns and goes to the king to petition for her house and land (wattēṣēʾ liszōq ʾel hammélek ʿal bêtāh wᵉʿal śādāh). The legal petition to the king for property restoration was the standard mechanism for resolving ownership disputes that arose during absence. In this case, she is seeking what she left behind when she obeyed the prophet's warning. Her obedience cost her seven years of land use; the question is whether the king's justice will restore it.
8.4: The king is speaking with Gehazi at this moment: wahammélek mᵉdabbēr ʾel Gēḥăzî naʿar ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm lēʾmōr sapĕrāh nāʾ lî ʾēt kol-haggᵉdōlôt ʾăšer ʿāśāh ʾĔlîšāʿ — tell me all the great things Elisha has done. The king's interest in Elisha's miracles at the exact moment the Shunammite woman enters creates a divinely timed confluence. The timing is not coincidental — YHWH's providential arrangement places her petition at the moment of maximum receptivity.
8.5: Gehazi is telling the story of the raised child: wayhî hûʾ mᵉsappēr lamélek ʾēt ʾăšer hehĕyyāh ʾet hammet wᵉhinnēh hāʾiššāh ʾăšer hehĕyyāh ʾet bᵉnāh ṣōʿaqat ʾel hammélek — and behold, the very woman whose son he raised comes crying to the king. The coincidence is a divine signature: the story being told and the storyteller of the story arrive simultaneously. hazzōʾt hāʾiššāh wᵉhazzeh bᵉnāh — this is the woman and this is her son. The living proof of the miracle is standing before the king as Gehazi testifies to it.
8.6: The king asks the woman, and she tells the whole story. He appoints an officer to restore her property: hāšēḇ ʾet kol-ʾăšer lāh wᵉʾēt kol-tᵉḇûʾôt haśśādeh mēyôm ʿāzᵉḇāh ʾet hāʾāreṣ wᵉʿad ʿattāh — restore everything that is hers, and all the produce of the field from the day she left until now. Full restitution including lost income — the king's justice is complete. The narrative demonstrates that obedience to prophetic warning is rewarded even years later with full restoration. The eschatological implication: those who sacrifice present possession for obedience to divine word receive full restitution (cf. Matt 19:29).
8.7: Elisha goes to Damascus while Ben-hadad king of Aram is sick (wᵉBen-Hadad melek ʾărām ḥōleh). Hazael is sent to consult the prophet: leḵ qᵉrēʾ layyᵉhûdî ûdrôš ʾet YHWH môtô miḥŏlî zeh — go, inquire of the Israelite (YHWH) whether I will recover from this illness. The Aramean king seeking a Hebrew prophet to inquire of YHWH echoes the Naaman narrative (ch5): YHWH's prophetic authority extends beyond Israel's borders. The Elisha-Damascus-Hazael sequence will determine the fate of Syria, fulfilling Elijah's original commission to anoint Hazael (1 Kgs 19:15).
8.8: Ben-hadad sends Hazael with a gift (wayyiqqaḥ minḥāh bᵉyādô) — forty camel-loads of all the good things of Damascus. The enormous gift-tribute reflects the Aramean king's desperation: he is dying and wants certainty about his fate. The minḥāh (gift, tribute) here is a diplomatic gift rather than a cultic offering — the gesture of a subordinate to a superior seeking information or favor. Elisha will receive the gift but give an answer the king did not expect.
8.9: Hazael comes and says: ʾāmarku bᵉnᵉḵā ʾăšer melek ʾărām Ben-Hadad ʾēlay ʾĕḥyeh mēḥŏlî zeh — your son (= vassal) Ben-hadad king of Aram has sent me to ask whether he will recover. ûḇinᵉḵā — “and your son” — the Aramean king addresses Elisha as “my father” by proxy, just as the king of Israel had done (6:21). The prophet's authority as spiritual father extends even to foreign kings.
8.10: Elisha says: lēḵ ʾĕmor lô ḥāyôh tiḥyeh wᵉhiggîd lî YHWH kî môt yāmût — go, say to him: “You will certainly recover” — but YHWH has shown me that he will certainly die. The apparent contradiction (will recover vs. will die) is resolved by Hazael's agency: Ben-hadad would recover from the illness, but he will be murdered by Hazael. Elisha knows not just the medical outcome but the political one. The prophetic word is not a simple prognosis but a complex disclosure of hidden intentions.
8.12: Hazael asks why Elisha weeps (cf. v11, present). Elisha answers: yādaʿtî ʾēt ʾăšer taʿăśeh liḇnê yiśrāʾēl rāʿāh miṣrôtêhem tᵉśallēaḥ bāʾēš wûḇḥurêhem tahărog baḥereḇ wᵉʿōlᵉlêhem tᵉrattēš wᵉhārōtêhem tᵉḇaqqēaʿ — I know the evil you will do to Israel: their strongholds you will set on fire, their young men you will kill with the sword, their infants you will dash in pieces, their pregnant women you will rip open. Elisha weeps because he sees the violence before it happens — the prophetic burden of seeing coming judgment while being unable to prevent it. Jesus weeps over Jerusalem for the same reason (Luke 19:41-44), knowing the destruction of 70 CE.
8.13: Hazael protests: kî mah ʿaḇdᵉḵā hakkeléḇ hammēt kî yaʿăśeh haddāḇār haggādôl hazzeh — what is your servant, a dead dog, that he should do this great thing? The self-deprecation is formally humble but structurally revealing: Hazael denies the capacity for such atrocity while already calculating it. Elisha responds: hirrʾānî YHWH ʾōtᵉḵā melek ʾărām — YHWH has shown me you will be king of Aram. The two prophecies are connected: the power to do these things requires kingship, and kingship is already prophetically assigned.
8.14: Hazael returns to Ben-hadad who asks: mah ʾāmar lᵉḵā ʾĔlîšāʿ — what did Elisha say? Hazael lies: wayyōʾmer lî ḥāyôh tiḥyeh — he told me you would certainly live. The lie preserves Hazael's position while enabling the murder. Hazael has just received a prophetic commission to greatness; he implements it through deception and violence — a pattern of human agency actualizing prophetic word through morally compromised means. The sovereignty of YHWH's decree and the responsibility of human sin coexist without the narrative resolving the tension.
8.15: The next day Hazael takes a thick cloth (hammakbēr), dips it in water, and spreads it over Ben-hadad's face until he dies. wayyimloḵ Ḥăzāʾēl taḥtāyw — Hazael reigns in his place. The murder fulfills both Elisha's prophecy (Hazael becomes king) and Elijah's original commission (1 Kgs 19:15: wᵉʾet ḥăzāʾēl timšaḥ lᵉmelek ʿal ʾărām). The kingship is bestowed through prophetic commission but actualized through regicide — YHWH's purposes are accomplished through violent human choices, which does not exonerate the human actor.
8.16: The Judean synchronism: Jehoram son of Jehoshaphat begins to reign over Judah in Joram son of Ahab's fifth year. Jehoshaphat was Judah's most faithful king after David and Solomon; his son Jehoram is its most faithless. The DH explains: wayyēlek bᵉdereḵ malkê yiśrāʾēl — he walked in the way of the kings of Israel. The marriage alliance with Ahab's house (v18) introduces northern-style Baalism into Judah. The pattern of the faithful parent followed by the apostate child repeats throughout Kings.
8.17: Jehoram of Judah's eight-year reign is noted. His age at accession (thirty-two) and eight-year reign are given without any positive evaluation — a stark contrast to the evaluations of most Judean kings. The absence of positive characterization in the regnal formula is itself a form of condemnation in the DH's evaluative framework.
8.18: wayyēlek bᵉdereḵ malkê yiśrāʾēl kaʾăšer ʿāśû bêt ʾAḥāḇ kî bat ʾAḥāḇ hāytāh lô lᵉʾiššāh — he walked in the way of the kings of Israel as the house of Ahab had done, for the daughter of Ahab was his wife. The marriage alliance between Jehoshaphat's heir and Ahab's daughter (Athaliah) introduces the Jezebel pattern into Jerusalem. The DH traces religious corruption through marriage alliances — Solomon's wives, Ahab's Jezebel, now Athaliah — consistent with Deuteronomy's warning against intermarriage with idolatrous peoples (Deut 7:3-4).
8.19: wᵉlōʾ ʾābāh YHWH lᵉhašḥît ʾet yᵉhûdāh lᵉmaʿan dāwid ʿaḇdô kaʾăšer ʾāmar lô lātēt lô nîr lᵉḇānāyw kol hayyāmîm — yet YHWH was not willing to destroy Judah for the sake of David his servant, as he promised to give him a lamp (nîr) for his sons always. The nîr (lamp, perpetual fire) for the Davidic dynasty is the covenant of 2 Sam 7 expressed as the preservation of the line despite apostasy. Judah's survival through idolatrous reigns is not merit-based but covenant-based — the unconditional Davidic promise. The NT reads this preservation as pointing to the ultimate Davidic heir in whom the covenant is finally and fully actualized (Luke 1:32-33; Rev 5:5).
8.20: During Jehoram's reign, Edom revolts from Judah's authority and installs its own king (wayyimloḵ ʿălêhem melek). The Edom revolt reverses the subjugation established under David (2 Sam 8:14). The loss of Edomite territory is a visible symptom of covenant infidelity: YHWH promised expansion and security for faithfulness; defections and revolts signal the withdrawal of that protection. Edom's eventual salvation is promised in the prophets (Amos 9:12 in its NT application via Acts 15:16-17) as part of the eschatological restoration of all nations.
8.21: Jehoram goes to Zair and strikes the Edomites who surround him — he breaks through at night (wayyaqqom laylāh wayyaḵ ʾet ʾĕdôm hasSōḇēḇ ʾēlāyw). The campaign is inconclusive: the army flees home (wayyānos hāʿām lᵉʾōhālāyw). A military breakout from encirclement is presented without clear victory — Jehoram escapes but does not reconquer. The political weakness is theological: apostate kings do not receive the full covenant-warrior protection.
8.22: wayyipšaʿ ʾĕdôm mittaḥat yad yᵉhûdāh ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — Edom revolted from the hand of Judah to this day. The narrator's “to this day” formula marks permanent political loss. The Libnah revolt mentioned in the same verse represents a second defection within the land — even a Judean city repudiates Jehoram's authority. The combined losses signal a kingdom contracting under divine judgment.
8.23: The closing formula for Jehoram of Judah: wᵉyeter diḇrê Yᵉhôrām wᵉḵol ʾăšer ʿāśāh hălōʾ hēm kᵉtûḇîm ʿal sēper diḇrê hayyāmîm lᵉmalkê yᵉhûdāh — the rest of Jehoram's acts are written in the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah. The source citation signals the court archive; Jehoram's full military and administrative record was available there.
8.24: wayyiškaḇ Yᵉhôrām ʿim ʾăḇōtāyw wayyiqqāḇēr ʾim ʾăḇōtāyw bᵉʿîr dāwid — Jehoram slept with his fathers and was buried with his fathers in the city of David. Ahaziah his son reigned in his place. The burial in the city of David continues even for apostate Judean kings — the Davidic city still receives its line. The burial is honorable in form but the narrative has made clear the king's apostasy.
8.25: Ahaziah son of Jehoram begins to reign over Judah — the synchronism is the twelfth year of Joram son of Ahab of Israel. Ahaziah's one-year reign makes him one of the shortest-lived Judean kings. His entire significance in the DH is as the fulcrum of the Jehu revolution that will destroy the house of Ahab and nearly extinguish the Davidic line (via Athaliah's usurpation in ch11).
8.26: Ahaziah of Judah's mother is Athaliah, daughter of Omri — ûšem ʾimmô ʿĂṯalyāhû bat ʿOmrî melek yiśrāʾēl. Athaliah is both the daughter of Omri (or granddaughter — 2 Chr 22:2 says daughter of Ahab) and the wife of Jehoram of Judah. The Omride connection runs through the queen mother into the Judean royal house. Athaliah's later seizure of the Davidic throne (ch11) makes this genealogical connection the pivot of Judah's near-extinction.
8.27: wayyēlek bᵉdereḵ bêt ʾAḥāḇ wayyaʿaś hāraʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH kᵉbêt ʾAḥāḇ kî ḥōtēn bêt ʾAḥāḇ hûʾ — he walked in the way of the house of Ahab and did evil in YHWH's eyes, as the house of Ahab had done, for he was a son-in-law (ḥōtēn) of the house of Ahab. The marriage alliance is the vehicle of theological corruption: Ahab's religious practices enter Judah through family connection. The DH's insistence that Ahaziah's evil is specifically modeled on Ahab's house creates a deliberate intertextual link between the parallel northern and southern apostasies.
8.28: Ahaziah goes with Joram son of Ahab to war against Hazael of Aram at Ramoth-gilead — the same front where Ahab died (1 Kgs 22). The Ramoth-gilead connection is deliberate: the city where Ahab met prophetically-announced death is now the site where the Ahab-dynasty connection begins its unraveling. Joram is wounded (wayyakkû ʾărammîm ʾet Yôrām) and returns to Jezreel to recover.
8.29: Ahaziah goes to Jezreel to visit Joram who was recovering from wounds received at Ramah. wᵉʾăḥazyāhû ben Yᵉhôrām melek yᵉhûdāh yārad lirʾôt ʾet Yôrām ben ʾAḥāḇ bîzrᵉʿel kî ḥōleh hûʾ. The visit to the recuperating king places Ahaziah at Jezreel at the moment Jehu's coup will begin (ch9-10) — the geography of the narrative is tightening around the coming judgment on the house of Ahab and its Judean ally. The convergence of both kings at Jezreel will be the setting for both their deaths.

12.2: The qualification of Joash’s fidelity — wayyaʿaś yôʾāš hayyāšār bᵉʿênê YHWH kol yāmāyw ʾăšer hôrāhû yᵉhôyāḏāʿ hakkōhēn, ‘Joash did what was right in the eyes of YHWH all his days because Jehoiada the priest instructed him’ — carries an embedded limit: the fidelity was conditional on Jehoiada’s instruction and Jehoiada’s life. When Jehoiada died (2 Chr 24:17-22), Joash turned to the princes of Judah and abandoned the house of YHWH; he even had Zechariah the prophet stoned when Zechariah rebuked him. The pattern — fidelity maintained under external guidance, abandoned when the guide is gone — is the OT’s recurring warning against faith that depends on human scaffolding. Heb 3:14 names the criterion for genuine faith: metochoi gar tou Christou gegonamen, eanper tēn archēn tēs hypostaseōs mechri telous bebaian katascho — ‘for we have come to share in Christ, if indeed we hold our original confidence firm to the end.’ Joash’s conditional fidelity (‘all his days [while] Jehoiada instructed him’) is the OT’s warning against the partial beginning that does not hold to the end — the contrast that Hebrews repeatedly addresses in its audience.
12.1: The accession notice places Jehoash within the Jehu dynasty of Israel, linking two kingdoms in one narrative frame. Every Judahite king is measured against David; none fully measures up until the king who is both son of David and Son of God. The forty-year reign is one of Judah's longer ones, yet the narrative gives more space to temple administration than military achievement — the house of YHWH is the real concern.
12.3: The regnal qualifier — "the high places were not removed; the people continued to sacrifice and burn incense at the high places" — appears with striking regularity for even reforming Judahite kings. Even Jehoash, who repairs the temple, cannot achieve complete cultic centralization. This recurrent failure reveals the limit of all human reform: the external structure can be improved, but the heart that seeks rival altars remains unchanged. Only Christ who gives a new heart (Ezek 36:26) achieves the complete purification that earthly kings never could.
12.4: Jehoash's temple repair plan — collecting consecrated money through the priests — reflects his genuine care for YHWH's dwelling. The temple as God's house requiring human maintenance is a shadow: the permanent dwelling of God with his people requires no such upkeep. Christ as the new temple (John 2:19–21) cannot be damaged by external force, nor does it need periodic repair from human hands.
12.5: The administrative arrangement — each priest receiving money "from his own contacts" — decentralizes the collection but leads to twenty-three years of inaction (v6). The plan's failure reveals the limits of institutional religion: structures exist to maintain God's house, but institutional inertia defeats them. The contrast with Christ's single atoning act, which permanently accomplishes what the temple system could not, is implied in the narrative's quiet critique.
12.6: Twenty-three years and no repair — a remarkable detail of institutional failure. The priests were entrusted with the house of God and did not act. The theme of unfaithful stewards runs through Scripture and reaches its sharpest expression in Jesus's parables of unfaithful servants (Matt 25:14–30; Luke 19:12–27). Christ himself undertook the cleansing and rebuilding of the true temple (John 2:16–19) without the twenty-three-year delay of human inertia.
12.7: Jehoash summons Jehoiada and rebukes the priests — royal authority exercised in defense of the temple's integrity. The pattern of a king correcting unfaithful priests anticipates the tension of the prophetic tradition and reaches its climax in Jesus's cleansing of the temple (Mark 11:15–17), where the messianic King exercises sovereign authority over the religious establishment that had turned his Father's house into something else.
12.8: The priests agree to give up accepting money in exchange for giving up responsibility for repairs — an administrative solution that shifts temple maintenance out of priestly control. The theme of divided labor between king and priest in maintaining God's house points forward to the one who unites both offices permanently: Christ as king who also serves as great high priest (Heb 7:17–26), making all such institutional arrangements obsolete.
12.9: The chest with a bored hole in its lid, placed beside the altar — a practical innovation to ensure transparency in temple offerings. The freewill gifts of the people flowing into the chest for the repair of God's house is a shadow of the community of the new covenant whose members contribute to the building up of the body of Christ (1 Cor 3:9; Eph 4:16). Both point to the desire of God's people to honor his dwelling.
12.10: The king's secretary and the high priest counting and bagging the money together — royal and priestly authority jointly overseeing temple resources. This dual oversight echoes the Zerubbabel-Joshua partnership after exile and anticipates the permanent union of kingly and priestly oversight in Christ. The careful accounting also points to the accountability that governs stewardship of what belongs to YHWH.
12.11: The counted money paid to workers — carpenters, builders, masons — who worked on the house of the LORD. The image of skilled craftsmen rebuilding God's house connects to Bezalel's tabernacle work (Exod 31) and Hiram's temple labor (1 Kgs 7). The new covenant community is described in similar terms: each member contributing different gifts to build up the body, which is the new temple (1 Cor 3:10–15; Eph 2:20–22).
12.12: The procurement of materials — timber and cut stone — for structural repairs to the house of the LORD. These are costly materials; the generosity of the offering sustains the building project. The theme of costly materials given for God's dwelling runs from the tabernacle through Solomon's temple; its fulfillment is Christ himself, who is both the costly gift and the permanent dwelling (John 1:14).
12.13: No silver bowls, snuffers, or trumpets were made from the repair money — it all went to structural work, not vessels. The distinction reveals a priority: the building's structure before its furnishings. The temple's vessels — instruments of worship — would be provided in due course, but the house itself must first stand. The new covenant's "house" (the body of believers) is similarly built up before its full glory is revealed.
12.14: The accountability system worked: the workers were trusted, the money was used for its stated purpose. The moral of the administrative detail is transparency and faithfulness in stewardship. The contrast with the earlier priestly failure (v6) is the text's implicit lesson: proper oversight enables faithfulness. Christ, the faithful steward who "received" all that the Father gave him and lost none (John 17:12; 18:9), is the ultimate fulfillment of trustworthy administration.
12.15: "They did not require an accounting from the men who received the money … because they dealt honestly." Trust extended because integrity was demonstrated. This snapshot of a moment when Israel's institutional life worked as it should — honest workers, trusted administrators — is a faint echo of the community governed by the Spirit, where the love of Christ shapes conduct without external compulsion (2 Cor 5:14).
12.16: The guilt and sin offering money was kept separate — it "belonged to the priests." The Levitical distinction between funds for temple maintenance and funds for the priestly income is preserved. The elaborate financial distinctions of the Mosaic system — maintained here in the midst of reform — anticipate the single, comprehensive atonement of Christ, who through one offering makes all such distinctions permanent history (Heb 10:12–14).
12.17: Hazael attacks Gath and threatens Jerusalem. The Aramean threat from the north, which had oppressed Israel, now turns against Judah. YHWH permits external enemies to press against his covenant people when they have not fully returned to him. The perpetual vulnerability of Jerusalem to surrounding powers under human kings contrasts with the city that "cannot be moved" whose king is YHWH himself (Ps 46:5) and with the heavenly Jerusalem that needs no defense because the Lord God is its light (Rev 21:23).
12.18: Jehoash buys off Hazael with the temple treasures — the consecrated gifts of Jehoshaphat, Jehoram, and Ahaziah, plus his own gifts, plus all the gold in the temple and royal treasury. The pattern of depleting the temple treasury to purchase military relief appears repeatedly in Judah's history (1 Kgs 15:18; 2 Kgs 16:8; 18:15). Sacred resources traded for temporary political safety — the inversion of covenant priorities. Christ does the reverse: he gives himself — the sacred treasure — so that his people might be permanently secure.
12.19: The regnal close formula — "the rest of the acts of Joash … are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?" — preserves the theological witness of the Davidic line for future readers. Every king in the line, however flawed, is recorded; the chronicle is kept because the line to Christ must not be lost. The faithfulness of YHWH in preserving the record is as much a Christological act as the content of any individual reign.
12.20: Jehoash is assassinated by his own servants at Beth-millo. The king who repaired the temple and bought off Jerusalem's enemies dies not in battle but by conspiracy — killed by those close to him. This pattern of the legitimate king betrayed and killed by intimate associates recurs in Israel's history and reaches its typological climax in Christ, delivered by one of the Twelve (Matt 26:14–16) and killed at the hands of those he came to serve (John 1:11).
12.21: The assassins are named: Jozachar son of Shimeath and Jehozabad son of Shomer. Their names are preserved; they are not anonymous. The detail serves the principle of accountability — the guilty are named. Yet burial occurs "with his ancestors in the city of David," which preserves the Davidic continuity despite violent death. YHWH's covenant with David is not broken by kings who die by violence; the line continues toward the one who rises from death permanently (Rom 1:4).

13.21: The dead man revived by contact with Elisha’s bones — thrown hastily into Elisha’s tomb and reviving when he touched them — is the OT’s solitary instance of life transmitted by a dead prophet’s remains. The narrative makes no theological claim about Elisha’s bones as a permanent source of power; it records a specific event without generalizing it into a relic-theology. The theological point is about the Spirit that worked through Elisha in life: even his dead bones are a conduit for the same Spirit that had worked through him. John 5:28-29 announces the ultimate extension of this principle: erchesthai hōra en hē pantes hoi en tois mnēmeiois akousontai tēs phōnēs autou kai ekporeusontai — ‘an hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out.’ The Elisha-bones miracle is the OT’s single narrative demonstration that the resurrection power that will raise all the dead is already present in the world in anticipatory form — that the prophetic Spirit is not contained by death. Heb 11:35 includes the OT resurrection episodes in the faith-hall: ‘women received back their dead by resurrection’ — and the Elisha-bones episode stands as the most posthumous of these, the resurrection-power persisting beyond even the prophet’s burial.
13.1: The synchronistic dating — "in the twenty-third year of Joash son of Ahaziah, king of Judah" — weaves the Northern and Southern narratives together into one redemptive timeline. Both kingdoms move through their respective histories within YHWH's single plan. The alternating narrative structure of Kings mirrors the biblical conviction that all history — including the history of fractured, failing kingdoms — is directed toward a single goal.
13.2: "He did evil in the sight of the LORD, following the sins of Jeroboam son of Nebat" — the standard Northern evaluation. Jehu's dynasty begins with promise (2 Kgs 10) and immediately reverts. The "sins of Jeroboam" — the golden calves at Bethel and Dan — are not merely political compromises; they are a rival liturgy, an alternative mediation of YHWH's presence. Only Christ removes the rival mediations permanently by making himself the sole mediator (1 Tim 2:5).
13.3: YHWH's anger burns against Israel and he gives them into Hazael's hand. The covenant curse operates: covenant unfaithfulness produces foreign domination. The mechanism is not arbitrary but covenantal — the very nations YHWH had Israel expel now return as his instruments of discipline. This pattern finds its ultimate resolution when Christ absorbs the covenant curse for his people (Gal 3:13), breaking the cycle of sin–discipline–incomplete repentance.
13.4: Jehoahaz "sought the favor (ḥillāh ʾet-pᵉnê) of the LORD, and the LORD heard him, for he saw the oppression of Israel." The gracious responsiveness of YHWH to even a compromised king's prayer is the theological center of the verse. "The LORD heard him" — YHWH as the God who hears, in contrast to the deaf idols of the nations (Ps 135:15–17). This compassion for the oppressed anticipates the incarnate Son who "heard" and came when there was no one left to help (v26).
13.5: môšîaʿ — "a deliverer" (H3467, hiphil participle of yāšaʿ) — YHWH gave Israel a deliverer so they escaped Aramean power. The identity of this deliverer is unnamed — possibly an Assyrian king who weakened Aram, or a later figure. The anonymity is theologically significant: the deliverer is YHWH's gift, not a human initiative. The term môšîaʿ is the same root as "Messiah" (māšîaḥ, anointed one) and "Jesus" (Yēšûaʿ); this deliverer is a type of the ultimate Deliverer who sets his people free from a power no human coalition can overcome.
13.6: "But they did not turn from the sins of the house of Jeroboam … they continued in them." Even after deliverance, no repentance follows. The pattern is painfully consistent: crisis produces prayer, prayer produces rescue, rescue produces continuation in sin. This cycle reveals the insufficiency of external deliverance without internal transformation. Christ's deliverance is not merely rescue from an enemy but the giving of a new heart (Jer 31:33; Ezek 36:26) that breaks the cycle permanently.
13.7: "For Hazael had left Jehoahaz no more than fifty cavalry, ten chariots, and ten thousand foot soldiers; the king of Syria had destroyed them and made them like the dust at threshing." Military diminishment as a consequence of apostasy. The tiny remnant of Israelite military force is the divine commentary on what trust in Jeroboam's calves produces. The people cannot defend themselves; only YHWH can save — and he does, through an unnamed deliverer (v5). The weakness of the people makes divine rescue the only available logic.
13.8: The regnal close for Jehoahaz — "are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?" — preserves the witness even of sinful Northern kings. The chronicle is kept, the testimony is maintained, because YHWH's purposes run through even corrupt lines. The habit of historical witness-keeping is itself a shadow of the faithfulness of YHWH who does not forget his people or his promises, however long the narrative of failure extends.
13.9: Jehoahaz "rested with his ancestors and was buried in Samaria; his son Joash reigned in his place." The brevity of the burial notice for a Northern king — no "city of David" for the North — marks the absence of the covenant burial ground. Samaria is not Jerusalem; the Northern dynasty lacks the covenantal geography of the South. Yet YHWH continues to work even through Northern kings, sustaining his purposes toward the one king who will be buried in Jerusalem and not remain buried (Matt 27:60; 28:6).
13.10: The accession of Jehoash (Joash) of Israel — "in the thirty-seventh year of Joash king of Judah" — another synchronistic link. The same name (Joash/Jehoash) for simultaneous kings of both kingdoms creates narrative complexity; the narrator navigates it carefully. Two kings with the same name, both failing differently: the Northern Jehoash fighting Amaziah of Judah, the Southern Joash assassinated. The fractured covenant community needs a king who unifies, not one who competes within it.
13.11: "He did evil in the sight of the LORD; he did not turn away from all the sins of Jeroboam son of Nebat." The formula repeats for each Northern king — a theological drumbeat of covenant failure. The monotony of the formula is itself the point: the structural problem of the Northern kingdom is not a fixable administrative failure but a root-level rejection of YHWH's mediation at Jerusalem. No internal reform can address a problem this deep; only the one who is himself both temple and sacrifice can resolve it.
13.12: The regnal summary notes Jehoash's military exploits — including his battle with Amaziah (ch14) — and closes. The Northern king's military success against Judah (ch14:11–14) is not celebrated but noted; it represents the tragic spectacle of God's covenant people fighting each other. The new covenant community, built on Christ's reconciling work, makes the warfare of the old order obsolete: "there is neither Jew nor Greek … for you are all one in Christ Jesus" (Gal 3:28).
13.13: Jehoash buried "in Samaria among the kings of Israel; his son Jeroboam sat on his throne." The dynasty continues into Jeroboam II, the most powerful of the Northern kings — who will also do evil. The persistence of the Jehu dynasty (four generations promised in 2 Kgs 10:30) is YHWH's faithfulness to his word even within a corrupt royal line. This fidelity to promises made even to sinful instruments reflects the same faithfulness that will sustain the Davidic line through all its failures until Christ.
13.14: Elisha's deathbed scene is among the most charged in the narrative. Joash king of Israel comes down to Elisha weeping: "My father! My father! The chariots of Israel and its horsemen!" — the same cry Elisha made at Elijah's ascent (2 Kgs 2:12). The prophet is Israel's true defense; the word of YHWH through the prophet is more effective than the entire military apparatus. This reaches its fulfillment in Christ, the Word made flesh, who is the ultimate defense of his people — not with chariots or cavalry, but by his death and resurrection.
13.15: Elisha instructs Joash to take bow and arrows — physical objects to enact a prophetic symbol. The prophet's use of concrete objects to embody divine promises runs through the prophetic tradition (Jeremiah's yoke, Ezekiel's siege-mime). The arrows become vessels of a divine promise; their proper use depends entirely on the king's faith and urgency. The capacity to receive the prophetic promise is the condition of its fulfillment.
13.16: Elisha places his hands on the king's hands as Joash draws the bow — the prophetic laying on of hands mediating divine power through human action. This is a concentrated image of prophetic intercession: the prophet's authority over the king's own, joined hands directing the shot. Christ, the prophet who is himself the word he speaks, does not merely lay hands on his people's actions — he inhabits them through his Spirit, enabling the action the human alone cannot perform.
